explicit_text: I was born in Fukushima Prefecture in 1899. It was my birthplace and where my early childhood was spent under the care of my grandparents after my father left for America when I was just five months old. Although I later moved to Tokyo for schooling, Fukushima remained the place of my roots before coming to America at age 18.

explicit_text: I went to Tokyo to attend Iwakuni Railroad School but had to switch to a high school because the school wasn't recognized by the Ministry of Education. Living in Tokyo gave me a perspective that made San Francisco seem quite familiar when I first arrived. My life in Tokyo was an important stepping stone before I decided to come to America.

explicit_text: I boarded the ship to America from Yokohama, where strict health examinations were conducted. I had to pass tests for Trachoma and roundworms, failing twice before finally passing. On my return trip to Japan to find a bride, I saw Yokohama full of young couples, which left a strong impression on me.

explicit_text: I arrived in San Francisco after passing the ship's health examinations. When I first met my father there, I recognized him from his photograph, but I was not impressed by the city since it reminded me of Tokyo's Marunouchi district. My father tried to show me around, hoping I would like it, but I remained indifferent.

explicit_text: During my voyage to America, the ship stopped in Honolulu where I experienced soda water for the first time. I also bought and ate bananas there, which was a memorable treat. Honolulu was a brief but vivid stop on my journey to San Francisco.

explicit_text: I came to Madera in 1920 where my family and I farmed land, including grapes and peaches. This was the place where the Shinwa Kai was organized in 1921 and the Japanese Language School was founded in 1923. Madera became the center of my community activities and farming life.

explicit_text: The Great Depression hit us hard after we divided our land in Madera. Many Japanese farmers sold their land and moved to Los Angeles, but my family decided to hold on despite the falling prices, especially for grapes. We struggled by growing vegetables and working very hard to survive through those difficult years.

explicit_text: Shinwa Kai was organized in Madera in 1921, and I served as its president. It was a key community organization for Japanese immigrants, and we celebrated New Year's in a storage room. Leading Shinwa Kai was part of my commitment to supporting our community.

explicit_text: At age 26, I was recommended as one of three directors of the Fresno Japanese Association, a position I held until the outbreak of the war. My duties included handling consulate work like death and marriage reports, mediating disputes, and lobbying against the California Alien Land Law. This role connected me closely with the Japanese community in Central California.

explicit_text: The California Alien Land Law of 1913 prevented us from buying land, so my father and his partner took various measures to evade it and acquire property. As a director of the Fresno Japanese Association, I worked to lobby against this law and support farmers affected by it. This law was a significant obstacle for Japanese immigrants trying to establish themselves.

explicit_text: During the Great Depression, many Japanese families sold their land and moved to the Los Angeles area because they could no longer make a living farming. Two of my children eventually moved to Los Angeles, while one son stayed here. Los Angeles represented a place of relocation for many in our community seeking new opportunities.
